messages: To change the app theme, open the app settings and select the theme you want to use.
Game plugin support is enabled by default.
Available app theme are listed here.
When you're finished, click the "Save" button to confirm your settings changes.

steps.description: Check for app updates on startup
messages: You can enable/disable app update checks on startup.

steps.description: Verify active profile on app startup
messages: You can enable/disable profile verification on startup.
You can disable this if profile verification is taking too long during startup.
This is the directory where profiles will be saved and loaded from.
The Steam installation folder. This is only required if Steam is installed to a non-standard location.
The default Proton version to use when launching games. May be overridden by profile.
You can enable NXM link handling in Stellar here. If enabled, Stellar will automatically handle mod manager downloads from Nexus Mods.
The current NXM link handling status is displayed here.
You can enter your Nexus Mods API key here. This is required for NXM link handling.
Your API key can be found here.
Active notifications are shown here.

You can activate and launch a profile directly from the CLI by using the --launch argument. For example, if you want to activate the profile named "My Profile" and launch the game, you can specify the following:

stellar-mod-loader.exe --launch "My Profile"

You can also optionally specify the game action to run:

stellar-mod-loader.exe --launch "My Profile" "Custom Action Name"

This will activate the given profile and invoke the given action (or the currently active action if one isn't specified).
You can check for new app updates by going to File > Check for Updates.
You can use the ? icons found throughout the app to get more information on a topic or setting.
Clicking these tooltips will show you guided help and explanations for different parts of the app.
You can view all of the available help topics by clicking Help > Show Topics in the menu bar at the top of the app.
If Stellar doesn't have built-in support for a game you want to mod, you can create a custom game definition to add support for it.
You can create game definitions and view existing game definitions by going to File > Manage Games.
Existing custom game definitions can also be imported or exported, allowing you to share game definitions with others or submit them for official inclusion into Stellar.
You can select a game to view here.
The ID of the game. It must be unique.
The title of the game.
The background color for the game. Can be either a hex color or rgb() definition.
The foreground color for the game. Can be either a hex color or rgb() definition.
The game installation paths for this game. These are typically the default installation folders for the game on supported platforms.
Click here to add a new game installation.
The root directory for the game installation.
The data directory for the game installation.
This may sometimes be the same as the root directory (in which case you can enter the value .).
The directory where the game's config files are located, if applicable. The location will vary by game.
The directory where game save files are located. The location will vary by game.
The location where the plugin list should be saved, if applicable.
The Steam IDs associated with this installation of the game, if any.
There may be more than one Steam ID associated with this game installation (i.e. GotY editions).
Whether or not the game uses multiple subdirectories for mod data.
This is often required for Unreal Engine games.
The list of game binaries associated with this game.
This list should also include binaries of known tools like editors and script extenders.
The list of save file formats associated with this game.
The list of plugin formats associated with this game, if any.
Whether or not external plugin file management is required for this game.
Click here to add a plugin data directory, if applicable for this game.
This directory defines where plugin files are located. This path is relative to the game's Data directory.
The directory where plugin files are located. This path is relative to the game's Data directory.
Add a plugin list for the game, if applicable.
The type of plugin list for the game.
This list should contain any plugin files that are required to be enabled for the game, if applicable.
The list of config files for this game, if applicable.
List any known script extenders for this game, if applicable.
The name of the script extender.
The names/paths of any binaries associated with the script extender.
List any special directories that are used by mods associated with this script extender.
If you want to see specific examples for any of these values, look through the built-in game definitions.

Once you are finished, click the "Save" button to confirm your changes.

The button will be disabled if there are any errors in your game definition.
Once saved, you can create a new profile using the new game definition.
To add a game, click the gear icon in Manage Games and select "Add New Game".
This allows you to use Stellar for other games that aren't officially supported.
To export a game, click the gear icon in Manage Games and select "Export Game". This allows you to export to a file and share your custom game definitions.

Once you have tested your game definition to make sure everything works as expected, feel free to open an issue and submit your exported game definition file for inclusion directly into Stellar.

If approved, your game definition will be added as a built-in game in a future release.
To import an existing custom game definition, click the gear icon in Manage Games and select "Import Game".
First, select the game you want to create this profile for.
Enter a name for your profile.
Navigate to the game's installation directory using the folder icon.
Game installations that have been automatically detected are listed here.
You can edit the game installation settings here.
You can edit the game installation settings here.
When you're finished, click the "Create" button to create the profile.
When you're finished, click the "Save" button to save your changes.
Base profiles are a special kind of profile that can be extended by other profiles.

Mods and plugins in a base profile are added and managed as normal, but cannot directly be deployed. Instead, other profiles can extend a base profile and will automatically inherit mods, plugins, and config files from the base profile.
This allows for defining a common set of mods that can be used and extended by other profiles.

One way this can be useful is for easily deploying a common set of mods between multiple PCs while also allowing for adding mods or config settings that may only be used on certain machines, such as high-res texture packs or specific compatability patches only needed for some devices.

This can also be used to sync mods between different versions of games, such as Skyrim SE and Skyrim VR.
Game directories are not defined for base profiles, allowing extending profiles to point to different game installation locations as needed.
To add an external profile, click the "Manage Profiles" button and select "Add External Profile".
An external profile will remain in the same directory it was added from, allowing for profiles to be used from any location or drive.
To add a new profile, click the "Manage Profiles" button and select "New Profile".
To import an existing profile, click the "Manage Profiles" button and select "Import Profile".
Importing a profile will create a new copy of the profile.
To export the current profile, click the "Manage Profiles" button and select "Export Profile".
Exported profiles will be removed from Stellar after successful export. A backup of the profile will be written to [OS temp dir]/SML before export.
To copy the current profile to a new profile, click the "Manage Profiles" button and select "Copy Profile".
To delete the current profile, click the "Manage Profiles" button and select "Delete Profile". This cannot be undone.
You can verify this profile by clicking the "Verify Profile" button.

Verification will check the profile's settings and files to make sure that there are no issues.

Any errors found during verification will be shown as an alert.

You can temporarily lock this profile by clicking the "Lock Profile" button.

When a profile is locked you will not be able to make changes to it.
You can view and change the profile's settings by clicking the "Profile Settings" button.

You can optionally pick a base profile to inherit from.

By default only base profiles from the current game are shown, but you can select any base profile by checkng Show all profiles.
You can edit the game installation settings here.
The root directory for the game installation.
The data directory for the game installation.

This may sometimes be the same as the root directory (in which case you can enter the value .).

For Bethesda games this is usually the Data folder.

The directory where the game's config files are located, if applicable. The location will vary by game.

For Bethesda games, this is located at [YourUserDirectory]/Documents/My Games/[GameName].

The directory where game save files are located. The location will vary by game.

For Bethesda games, this is located at [YourUserDirectory]/Documents/My Games/[GameName]/Saves.

The location where the plugin list should be saved, if applicable.

For Bethesda games, this is located at [YourUserDirectory]/AppData/Local/[GameName]/plugins.txt.
The Steam IDs associated with this installation of the game, if any. This is only applicable if you're using a Steam version of the game.
You can set Proton settings here.
The version of Proton to use when launching games with this profile.
The Proton prefix directory to use for this profile. Defaults to game's Steam compatdata prefix if found, otherwise a new prefix will be created in the profile folder.
You can optionally change the profile's default directories.
The root directory is where the profile's data is stored. By default, profiles are stored in the profiles directory defined in the app preferences.

Overriding this path will allow you to store the profile at an alternative location. This can be useful if your game is installed on a different drive and you want to co-locate the profile to the same drive as the game to enable Link mode.
Overriding this path will allow you to store the profile's mods at an alternative location. By default, mods are stored in the profile's root directory under mods.
Overriding this path will allow you to store the profile's game saves at an alternative location. By default, saves are stored in the profile's root directory under save.
This can be useful if you are syncing game saves between multiple PCs or profiles.
Overriding this path will allow you to store the profile's config files at an alternative location. By default, saves are stored in the profile's root directory under config.
This can be useful if you are syncing config files between multiple PCs or profiles.
Overriding this path will allow you to store the profile's backup files at an alternative location. By default, saves are stored in the profile's root directory under backups.

When Link Mode is enabled, file links to the mod files will be used instead of copying the files when activating the profile. This is significantly faster and uses less disk space. This setting is recommended to be enabled when possible.

Link mode can also be separately enabled for config files and save files. To enable/disable link mode for config or save files, click the icon to the left of Game Config Files Directory or Game Saves Directory.
Link mode can only be enabled if the profile is located on the same disk/partition as the game itself. You can change the profile's location by changing the Profile Root Path under Path Overrides.
If the Manage Config/INI Files option is enabled, new config files will be created for the profile. This requires the game installation to have a valid Game Config Files Directory.
Upon first enabling the option, you will be prompted to copy the existing config files from the Game Config Files Directory to the profile's config files.

When activating mods, the profile's config files will be copied to the Game Config Files Directory.

If any existing config files are in the Game Config Files Directory when mods are activated, they will be moved to a .sml.bak folder during activation. The files moved to .sml.bak will be restored back to their original location upon deactivating mods.
If save management is enabled, any game saves made while the profile is activated will be saved to the profile. These saves will only be visible in the game when the associated profile is activated.
This requires the game installation to have a valid Game Saves Directory.

Some mods may use different casing for their files/folders (i.e. "Interface" vs "interface") and this can cause issues on case-sensitive file systems (often used on Linux). When this setting is enabled, Stellar will automatically convert all activated mod files and folders to the correct case to avoid this issue.
It is recommended to enable this setting when using a case-sensitive file system.
Archive invalidation is required in some games in order to load certain kinds of mods, like texture packs and mesh replacers. Enabling this is recommended.
It is recommended to also enable the "Manage Config/INI Files" option. However if it is disabled, and existing config files can be located, archive invalidation can still be enabled.
To add a new mod, click the + button and select "Add Mod".
You can also add mods by dragging and dropping them into the app. You can drag and drop multiple mods to sequentially install them.
If you want to add a mod from a folder instead of an archive, click the + button and select "Import Mod".
To download a mod from a URL, click the + button and select "Download Mod".

Root mods are mods that are deployed to the Game Base Directory instead of the Mod Base Directory.

This allows for script extenders, DLSS injectors, ENBs, and other types of injectors to be managed as mods in your profile.
To add a new root mod, click the + button and select "Add Mod".
During installation, you will have the option to add the mod as a root mod.
You can create section dividers in your mod list to better keep mods organized.
To add a new section to the mod list, click the + button and select "Add Mod Section".
You can rename or delete a section divider by right clicking the section and selecting the desired option. All mods in a section can also be enabled or disabled.
Mods can be easily moved between sections or to the top/bottom of a section by right clicking a mod and opening the Section menu.
To begin, choose a mod to add.
You can adjust the mod name here.
You can set a version number here.
You can install the mod as a root mod.
You can add or remove mod files here. By default, all files from the mod will be added.
Use the checkboxes to keep or remove files and directories.
You can mark a directory as the root data directory. This will result in only the files inside this directory being added.
Some mods may be packaged in a way that you need to adjust the root directory. For Bethesda games, you should set the Data directory as the root directory.

Multiple directories can be marked as a root data directory if needed. This is useful for some mod formats like BAIN, where directories that start with numbered prefixes (00, 10, 20, etc) represent features that can be optionally installed.

To choose which features are installed, mark the features you want to install as root data dirs. Only these features will be installed.

Some games will use multiple directories for mods. In this case, Stellar will attempt to figure out where to install mods if possible, but some mods may be packaged in a way where this is not possible.

If Stellar cannot determine the installation directory, you will need to choose which folder to install the mod to.
If no folder is selected, the mod will be installed to the top level data (root) directory.
Use the arrow buttons to view child directories.

Some mods are packaged with special metadata known as FOMOD that allows for customizing the installation through a guided flow.

Stellar supports FOMOD and will automatically show the installation wizard for FOMOD-compatible mods.
The installer will guide you through the installation of the mod.
Hovering over or selecting an option will show information about what it does.
Some mods will ask you if you are using Vortex or Mod Organizer 2. Stellar supports either option, but if you encounter any issues, select Mod Organizer 2.
You can click the preview image (or the ? tooltip in compact view) to show a fullscreen view of the image.
Use the Next and Previous buttons to navigate between install steps.
You can install the mod as a root mod.
If you want to install the mod manually instead, you can click the Manual Install button at the bottom left corner of the installer window.
Once you have completed all required steps, click the Install button to install the mod.

Mods you have added will appear in your mods list with the load order of that mod shown to the right of its name.

You can modify the load order of a mod by dragging and dropping it in the list.
Unchecking a mod will disable it and make it inactive.
Mods inherited from a base profile cannot be re-ordered or enabled/disabled.
You can right click a mod to bring up additional actions, such as renaming or deleting.
You can browse the mod's files by clicking "View in File Explorer".
You can move mods within the current section under the "Section" menu.
You can move a mod to the start of the load order by clicking "Move to Top".
You can move a mod to the end of the load order by clicking "Move to Bottom".
You can check for updates for a mod by clicking "Check for Updates". This is only available for mods from known sources.
You can click "Copy URL" to copy the URL of the mod to your clipboard. This is only available mods downloaded through Stellar.
You can rename a mod by clicking "Rename mod".
You can delete a mod by clicking "Delete mod". This cannot be undone.
You can customize which columns of the mods list are visible under the View > Mod List Columns section of the app menu bar.
You can add a mod by clicking the "Add Mod" button.
You can explore mod files for this profile by clicking the "Explore Profile Mod Files" button.
You can backup and restore the mod load order using the buttons at the top of the mod list.
Clicking the "Create Mod Order Backup" button will allow you to create a new mod order backup.
Clicking the "Restore Mod Order Backup" button will show a list of all available mod order backups that can be restored.
Note that any mods in a backup that are not currently added to the profile will not be re-added.
Existing game files and other files that have been manually copied to the Mod Base Directory outside of your profile will show up in the UI as External files.

When activating mods for a profile that overwrite external files, the original external files will be moved to a folder called .sml.bak while mods are activated.

The files in the .sml.bak folder will be restored back to their original location upon deactivating mods.
You can see which files are being overwritten by specific mods by enabling mod overwrite calculations.
To enable mod overwrite calculations, click the file icon next to the Mod Name header in the mod list.
Once overwrite calculations are enabled, file icons will be displayed next to mods that overwrite files from other mods or external files.
Clicking the file icon will show the list of files that the mod is overwriting.
To enable mods in the game you must first deploy your profile. Press the Activate Profile button in the Actions section to deploy the current profile.
Mods will remain active until you click Deactivate Profile button, even if you close the app or restart your PC.

IMPORTANT NOTE: If Link Mode is not enabled for the profile and you update any of the profile's mod files externally (i.e. in a text editor) while mods are deployed, make sure to press the Refresh Files button after making your changes.

Otherwise your changes will not be applied.
You can check for updates for mods that have been downloaded through Stellar from a known source like Nexus Mods.
To check for updates, right click a supported mod and click "Check for Updates".
You can view all new versions of the mod and choose which version to install.
Each version's information is listed here.
You can also view the changelog for each version, if available.
Click here to download the update.
You can view older versions of a mod by clicking the "Show Old Versions" button.
Once at least one mod with a plugin has been installed, you will see your plugins listed along with their load order.

Plugins can be individually disabled or re-ordered by dragging and dropping them.

Plugins inherited from a base profile cannot be re-ordered or enabled/disabled.
You can right click individual plugins to bring up additional options.
You can move a plugin to the start of the load order by clicking "Move to Top".
You can move a plugin to the end of the load order by clicking "Move to Bottom".
Some games have rules regarding the load order of different types of plugins.

For Bethesda games, the order is generally ESM -> ESL -> ESP. However, mods sometimes contain ESP plugins that are "flagged" as ESM or ESL.

While Stellar does not currently parse these flags, you can manually account for this by using plugin type promotion.

To promote a plugin to a new type, right-click it and use the "Plugin Type" option to select the new plugin type.

Once promoted, the plugin will behave as if it were of the promoted type.

External game plugin management is optional for some games and is required for others. For games where external plugin management is optional, it can be enabled or disabled by pressing the "Manage External Plugins" button at the top left of the plugins list.
When enabled, all external game plugin files will be shown in the list and can be re-ordered alongside profile-managed plugins.
You can backup and restore the plugin load order using the buttons at the top right of the plugins list.
Selecting the "Create Plugin Backup" option will allow you to create a new plugin order backup.
Selecting the "Restore Plugin Backup" option will show a list of all available plugin order backups that can be restored.
Note that any plugins in a backup that are not currently in the active plugin list will not be re-added.
If you want to import a plugin order backup from another profile, use the "Import Plugin Backup" button.
You can also export the plugin order in a plugins.txt-compatible format using the "Export Plugins List" button.
If you have enabled the Manage Config/INI Files option for your profile, you can select the "Config" option from the dropdown near the top right area of the app.
From this section you can edit your profile-specific config/INI files.
You can browse the config files for the profile by selecting them from the dropdown.
Click the "Save" button to save any changes.
You can reload the config file in case changes were made externally by clicking the "Reload file contents" button.
You can open the config file in an external editor by clicking the "Open in external editor" button.
You can also delete config files from the profile.
You can explore the profile's config file folder by clicking the "Open Profile Config Folder" button.
You can backup and restore the current config file values using the buttons at the top right of the Config editor.
Selecting the "Create Config Backup" option will allow you to create a new config file backup.
Selecting the "Restore Config Backup" option will show a list of all available config file backups that can be restored.
If you have enabled the Manage Save Files option for your profile, you can select the "Saves" option from the dropdown near the top right area of the app.
From this section you can manage your profile-specific save files.
You can view details about each save game managed by this profile.
You can delete saves by clicking the delete button.
Use the "Refresh Files" button to refresh the list.
You can explore the profile's saves folder by clicking the "Open Profile Saves Folder" button.
You can either click the Start Game button or simply launch the game directly through Steam, Game Pass, etc. The game should launch with your mods enabled!
Some game actions can be run with different launchers. If an action has multiple available runners, you can change which runner to use from the menu to the left of the button.
Some games and script extenders may have additional launch options. These will appear in the dropdown to the right of the button.
From here you can select which game action to launch.
Custom actions can be added by clicking the dropdown arrow to the right of the launch action button (Start Game) and clicking New Action.
You must give the action a name.
Choose what kind of action to create.
For actions that use Proton, use can have this action use a specific Proton version.
Enter the program or script to run.
Enter the Steam App ID to run.
You can add environment variables to command-based actions.
Click the "Show Macros" button to view available macros.
Runs a script or executable, can include arguments and environment variables.
Launches an app or shortcut from Steam using its Steam App ID.

One way the Steam App ID can be found by adding a desktop shortcut for the app in Steam and then inspecting the shortcut file. The shortcut will have an argument in the format of steam://rungameid/13109217361204871168 where the App ID is the number at the end.

(Linux) Launches a script or executable using steam-launch-wrapper to invoke Proton.

Steam must be installed on your system.

(Linux) Launches a script or executable using umu-launcher to invoke Proton.

umu-launcher must be installed on your system.
Actions, such as activating your profile and launching the game, are listed in the Actions panel.
You can browse your profile by clicking the "Profile Folders" button.
You can browse game folders by clicking the "Game Folders" button.
If profile-managed config/INI files are disabled, you will see an option in Actions to view external game config files.
You can see if your mods are active here. This will also tell if you if mods from another profile are enabled for the current game.
The currently selected action is shown here. Click this button to run the action.
By default, you will see an action to launch the game.
You can also create custom actions and modify existing actions.
You can also edit an existing action by clicking the Edit button (gear icon) next to the action.
You can export custom actions to your Steam Library.
Click the dropdown arrow to the right of the launch action button and click the gear icon next to a custom action.
Click the Create Steam shortcut button at the top right of the action settings window to create the current action as a shortcut in your Steam library.
The download queue allows you to manage your mod downloads.
You can view information about each download. Download progress is shown for active downloads.
You can use the Auto-install downloads button to control whether or not downloaded mods are auto-intalled as they are downloaded.
You can use the Show Downloads Folder button to view download files for this profile.
Click the Actions icon (three dots) to the left of a download to view the available actions.
Begins mod installation for a completed download.
Shows the download in the file explorer.
Cancels pending or active downloads.
Removes and deletes the download.
Retry a failed download.
Removes a failed download.
Click the Bulk Actions icon at the top left of the download queue to view all bulk actions.
This action will delete all downloads that have been completed. This cannot be undone.
This action will delete all downloads regardless of status. This cannot be undone.
You can change the sort order of the downloads in the queue by clicking any of the header labels.
